title: Architectural Patterns: Moving Beyond Cloud-Native to Local-First - Insights from Adam Wiggins
gist: Heroku co-founder Adam Wiggins argues local-first architecture (CRDT-based sync engines, exemplified by Linear) has matured from academic prototype to production-grade infrastructure correcting an over-rotation to 'everything in the cloud,' and predicts AI will follow the same pendulum: small local models handling ~80% of routine tasks with large cloud LLMs reserved for high-compute work, driven by connectivity fragility, geopolitical data-center risk, and user demand for offline agency.

themes.theme: Local-first as a correction, not a rejection, of cloud

insights.claim: Local-first architecture is not a rejection of the cloud but a necessary correction to the 'everything-in-the-cloud' paradigm, prioritizing offline capability and latency while still using centralized coordination for collaboration.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Frames local-first not as an anti-cloud investment thesis but as a hybrid-architecture opportunity — relevant for evaluating sync-engine and edge-compute startups as complements to, not replacements for, hyperscaler cloud spend.
insights.quote: Local-first is not a rejection of the cloud, but a necessary correction to the "everything-in-the-cloud" paradigm, prioritising offline capability and latency.
insights.locator: Key Takeaways

insights.claim: Running Heroku exposed Wiggins to the operational cost of centralizing everything: even a few seconds of downtime for a mission-critical enterprise app angered users losing money or facing business trouble, prompting him to question whether every user action needs to route through complex centralized infrastructure for something as simple as checking a checkbox.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: First-hand founder experience linking centralized-cloud downtime directly to lost revenue and customer anger — a grounded rationale for enterprises to evaluate local-first architectures for latency-critical, simple operations.
insights.quote: it seems odd that we've created such a big stack of things that can go wrong for such simple operations, like checking a checkbox on a to-do list
insights.locator: Why Local First Matters section

insights.claim: Geopolitical instability and data-center outages (referencing recent months of AWS-related disruptions) are increasing the operational and business risk of full dependence on centralized cloud infrastructure, particularly for European/banking customers already navigating heavy regulation after migrating off legacy on-prem systems.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Directly links geopolitical/infrastructure fragility to enterprise architecture decisions — a signal to watch for increased enterprise demand for local-first/hybrid resilience patterns, especially in regulated European sectors.
insights.quote: if AWS catches a cold, the whole internet is sneezing, and we saw that several times in the last couple of months
insights.locator: mid-interview exchange on cloud dependency

themes.theme: CRDTs and sync engines have crossed into production maturity

insights.claim: CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) and the sync engines built on them have matured from bleeding-edge, academic-only technology in the late 2010s/early 2020s to a level of maturity Wiggins would now bet a business on, for a specific set of use cases.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A direct signal from a credentialed infrastructure founder that CRDT-based sync-engine startups are now investable/adoptable technology, not research bets — relevant timing signal for the sync-engine vendor category.
insights.quote: Technologies like CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) have matured sufficiently to move from academic prototypes to robust, production-grade infrastructure, as evidenced by tools such as Linear.
insights.locator: Key Takeaways

insights.claim: Linear, the ticket-tracking software now used across Fortune 500 companies, achieves its perceived speed by writing every update (comments, status changes) first to local storage (e.g., IndexedDB in-browser) with instant UI update, then syncing to the server via a background thread — not via optimistic UI faking a response.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A concrete, named case study (Linear) of a sync-engine architecture delivering a widely noticed perceived-speed advantage — a reference implementation for evaluating competing project-management/collaboration tools.
insights.quote: It really is writing into your local storage, and then there's a background thread that's perpetually syncing that with the server.
insights.locator: Why Local First Matters, Linear discussion

insights.claim: Conflict resolution for CRDT-based sync has been an active computer science subfield for roughly 15 years and has produced technical solutions that perform much better than intuition would suggest, though suitability still depends heavily on domain (e.g., works well for Linear's team-sized datasets, but banking is cited as a domain where it likely would not work as well).
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A domain-suitability filter for evaluating local-first/CRDT adoption: works for bounded, team-scale collaborative datasets; likely unsuitable for domains requiring strict consistency like banking — directly useful for sector-specific technology risk assessment.
insights.locator: Why Local First Matters, conflict resolution discussion

insights.claim: Local-first adoption is not typically all-or-nothing across a software stack; the more common and successful pattern is adapting specific parts of an application to be local-first (using sync engines where latency/UX benefits are highest) while keeping the server in an almost-central coordinating role for the rest.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Actionable adoption framework: evaluate local-first at the feature/subsystem level rather than as a wholesale architecture migration — reduces perceived implementation risk for enterprise buyers.
insights.quote: it's not that you make the whole software stack local-first, but there's parts of it... this can be adapted to get you those business benefits
insights.locator: Why Local First Matters, closing framework

themes.theme: Files, ownership, and the SaaS unplug risk

insights.claim: Classic local files gave users a sense of control and agency (knowing that deleting a file truly deletes it, being able to freely copy/backup/experiment) that has been lost in the fully cloud-hosted era, and this loss is a distinct motivating force behind local-first alongside the operational/latency argument.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Identifies user-agency/data-ownership as a separate demand driver (distinct from performance) for local-first products — relevant for positioning products around privacy/ownership rather than just speed.
insights.quote: There was this sense of control, ownership, and just agency that came with files.
insights.locator: Why Local First Matters section

insights.claim: SaaS products that get shut down or 'unplugged' typically leave users with only a short window (e.g., 30 days) to extract data, and most non-technical users don't know what to do with an exported data blob, illustrating a systemic data-ownership failure of the pure-cloud SaaS model.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A concrete risk pattern (SaaS shutdown = stranded, unusable data for most users) that strengthens the business case for data-portability and local-first standards, and is a real customer-retention/reputational risk for SaaS vendors.
insights.quote: most usual users don't know what to do with it. What should I do if they get it? It's just a blob that is not actually the one they needed.
insights.locator: The Balance of Cloud and Local Collaboration, discussion of SaaS shutdowns

insights.claim: Git and GitHub demonstrate that local-first and cloud-centralized models are not mutually exclusive: Git gives engineers full local history and offline capability while GitHub adds centralized collaboration — a hybrid pattern Wiggins holds up as the template for how other domains (documents, spreadsheets) should evolve.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Git/GitHub is repeatedly cited as the proof-of-concept hybrid model — a template investors can use to evaluate whether new local-first products in non-code domains (docs, spreadsheets) are replicating this successful pattern.
insights.quote: I don't think it has to be either-or exactly like that. Git and GitHub, I think, show that.
insights.locator: The Balance of Cloud and Local Collaboration section

themes.theme: Version control primitives are an untapped market beyond code

insights.claim: Version control (branching, merging, diffing — the Git/GitHub model) is a foundational tool for all creative work, but today only software engineers have real access to it; extending pull-request-equivalent primitives to spreadsheets, word processors, and calendars for ordinary (non-engineer) users is identified as the next major productivity leap.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A specific, named whitespace opportunity ('Git for everyone') that Ink & Switch is actively researching — a thesis for productivity-tooling investors to track: which startups are bringing branch/merge/diff primitives to non-code artifacts.
insights.quote: The next major productivity leap will occur when we bring Git-like version control primitives (branching, merging, diffing) to non-code domains like documents and spreadsheets.
insights.locator: Key Takeaways

insights.claim: Existing non-engineer version-control analogues are primitive by comparison (e.g., legal redlining, basic version history in some office tools), and Ink & Switch's research question is whether these primitives can be made accessible enough for anyone working in a spreadsheet or word processor.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Specifies the current baseline (redlining, shallow version history) that any new product in this space needs to beat — a concrete competitive benchmark.
insights.locator: Software for More Productive Humans section

themes.theme: Local-first AI: small local models for routine work, cloud for heavy compute

insights.claim: The future of AI integration lies in small, high-performance local models handling roughly 80% of routine productivity tasks, with large cloud-based LLMs reserved for high-compute tasks, mirroring the local-first data pattern of local nodes plus central coordination.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified prediction (~80/20 split of routine vs. heavy-compute AI tasks between local and cloud models) with direct implications for on-device AI chip demand (Apple Silicon, local NPUs) versus hyperscaler GPU/TPU cloud compute demand.
insights.quote: The future of AI integration lies in small, high-performance local models that handle 80% of routine productivity tasks, with large cloud-based LLMs reserved for high-compute tasks.
insights.locator: Key Takeaways

insights.claim: Current AI tooling design fully centralizes work into expensive GPU compute clusters mainly because the field is still new and hasn't yet learned to properly deploy small open-weight models for these problems — implying this is a temporary, not permanent, state of the market.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A contrarian-adjacent view against the current 'send everything to giant cloud GPU clusters' consensus — implies future compute demand may fragment away from pure hyperscaler GPU cluster dependence as local model tooling matures.
insights.quote: because the field is still so new, it's just easier to throw everything into the big, expensive GPU compute clusters and kind of send everything to one place
insights.locator: How Will The AI Agents' Space Evolve? section

insights.claim: The anecdote of a developer frustrated on a flight because he 'didn't have access to Claude' illustrates current over-dependence on cloud AI models for coding workflows; Wiggins predicts this evolves toward harnesses that seamlessly switch between local and cloud models depending on connectivity and task, similar to the 'pie agent framework' being showcased at Local-First Conf.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Names a specific emerging framework category (agent harnesses that switch between local/cloud models by connectivity) to watch as a bridge technology between today's cloud-only coding assistants and a hybrid local/cloud future.
insights.quote: there's stuff I can do. I think there's a version of that ahead for local models
insights.locator: How Will The AI Agents' Space Evolve? section

insights.claim: High-end MacBooks now function as powerful local servers, reversing the earlier Chromebook-era trend of thin, cloud-dependent client devices — an indicator that local compute capacity is again becoming a meaningful differentiator as AI workloads move client-side.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Supports a thesis that premium local-compute hardware (Apple Silicon-class devices) gains relevance as AI-workload distribution shifts away from thin-client/all-cloud models — relevant for hardware demand forecasting.
insights.quote: if you look at the top of the line in terms of MacBooks, you do have a very powerful server on your desk, and it's pointless to use only browsers
insights.locator: exchange following AI agents discussion

insights.claim: GPUs were not originally designed for LLM/agent memory-access workloads, and the industry is moving toward TPUs as a more purpose-built refinement — framed as part of a continuous hardware evolution relevant to how local vs. cloud AI compute gets allocated.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A secondary hardware-architecture claim (GPU-to-TPU shift for AI memory workloads) raised by the interviewer and endorsed in the flow of conversation — worth tracking as a directional signal on AI compute hardware evolution, though attributed to the interviewer rather than Wiggins himself.
insights.quote: GPUs were not considered for this kind of load, and then they have all those problems... now, if you look at it, we are moving towards the TPUs that are closer, it's a refinement of the GPUs
insights.locator: exchange on GPUs and TPUs

themes.theme: Local-First Conf as a bellwether community and Europe/US dynamics

insights.claim: The term 'local-first' was coined in a 2019 essay after years of prior academic research; Local-First Conf held its first edition in Berlin in 2024 (sold out, under 200-person venue), doubled in size for its second edition, and expects nearly 400 attendees for its third edition in mid-2026 — evidence of rapid, compounding community growth from niche to mainstream industry interest.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A concrete attendance growth trajectory (sold out <200 in 2024, doubled in 2025, ~400 expected in 2026) that serves as a quantifiable adoption/interest curve for the local-first movement as a whole — useful as a leading indicator for VC interest in the space.
insights.quote: we have a venue... suitable space to hold the almost 400 people we expect to be in attendance this year
insights.locator: The Local First Community is Growing section

insights.claim: The conference's technical focus has shifted over three years from establishing whether a cross-disciplinary community existed at all (year 1), to deep technical focus on CRDTs and sync engines built by venture-funded startups (year 2), to broadening into adjacent areas like identity/authentication, the AT Protocol community, and open-source/privacy/encryption communities while addressing AI's role (year 3).
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Signals that the local-first ecosystem's center of gravity is broadening beyond pure sync-engine tech into identity/auth and decentralized-protocol territory (AT Protocol) — relevant adjacent categories for investors tracking this space.
insights.locator: The Local First Community is Growing section

insights.claim: Multiple venture-funded companies are now building sync engines or sync-adjacent technology, and their products have matured and become more usable over the past year, according to Wiggins' direct observation from organizing the conference.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: Direct confirmation from an insider that VC capital is already flowing into the sync-engine category and that products are maturing — a timing signal that the category is past the earliest-stage risk window.
insights.quote: We have a lot of new companies, in many cases venture-funded companies, that are building sync engines
insights.locator: The Local First Community is Growing section

insights.claim: Wiggins argues Europe has strong potential to be a tech and economic powerhouse but suffers a structural weakness where growing companies often must found a US entity and take US venture capital, or founders relocate to the US entirely — citing the EU Inc initiative (led by Andreas Klinger) as an attempt to fix this bureaucratic/regulatory gap.
insights.speaker: Adam Wiggins
insights.so_what: A specific, named policy effort (EU Inc) to watch as a potential unlock for European startup capital formation — relevant for investors tracking European tech competitiveness and startup jurisdiction arbitrage.
insights.quote: you do see this migration that when a company gets serious, they either need to go found a US entity and take venture capital or in some cases, the founders move to the United States, and that seems like a really, really big missed opportunity
insights.locator: closing discussion on Europe
